subject: [PATCH v2] mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledges a potential performance regression due to increased iteration over pages in pcpu allocation, plans to run further tests to assess impact.

Author acknowledged that stashing per-node counters per-chunk doesn't work well for statistics per-pcpu allocation, and plans to revise the patch.
reviews.raw_body:
I haven't done any performance analyses, but the changes that were made required
every pcpu allocation to iterate over all the pages in a loop, and account the
page where it came from, whereas previously we didn't need to do any iteration,
just charging or uncharging based on the size. But maybe it's not so bad
after all, since these allocations should usually be pretty small.

Let me try running some tests to see what the absolute worst case scenario
regression would look like.

---

The other approach I tried to do to avoid the iteration over pages was to
stash per-node counters per-chunk. But of course this doesn't work well if
we need to have statistics per-pcpu allocation, or if we change the ordering
of the charges based on the ordering of the chunk's pcpu allocations.

In any case, thanks for taking the time to check on the patch.
I'll try to spin up something soon!

Joshua

2026-04-15.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory accounting system to provide more detailed information about per-memcg-lruvec (per-cgroup, per-node, and per-LRU vector) percpu allocations. It does this by converting a certain type of memory tracking from a per-memcg level to a per-node level, allowing for better visibility into memory pressure on multi-NUMA systems. The patch also introduces separate accounting for node-level statistics and memcg-lruvec statistics, which requires exposing a new function to be used outside of the memory control module. This change improves the kernel's ability to track memory usage at a finer granularity, particularly for percpu allocations, but has a small memory overhead and negligible performance impact.
